featureInventory.feature: Speaker registration and authentication (email/phone + bcrypt + JWT)
Prompt catalogue — multi-language categorised text prompts for speakers to read aloud (21 categories, 9 emotions)
featureInventory.evidence: prisma/schema.prisma:PromptCategory enum, PromptEmotion enum; src/app/api/v2/speaker/prompts/ route

featureInventory.feature: Speaker recording upload with per-speaker labels (speaker_NNNN) and LJSpeech-compatible file paths
Pluggable storage abstraction — Google Cloud Storage (production) or local filesystem (development) via StorageProvider interface
Audio review workflow — PENDING_REVIEW → PENDING_TRANSCRIPTION approval pipeline
Transcription assignment and claim system with expiry (15-minute TTL, 3 max concurrent)
Transcription submit and flag mechanism (flag reasons: NOISE, UNCLEAR, TOO_QUIET, WRONG_LANGUAGE)
Reviewer transcription approval/rejection workflow
featureInventory.evidence: src/app/api/v2/reviewer/recordings/ (PENDING_REVIEW recordings returned with signed URLs); tests: src/app/__tests__/api/v2/reviewer/

featureInventory.feature: Multi-role user system: SPEAKER, TRANSCRIBER, REVIEWER, ADMIN (users can hold multiple roles simultaneously)
Dataset export in LJSpeech format (JSON and CSV) with speaker anonymisation via speaker labels
AI-powered English correction suggestions for transcribers (OpenAI GPT-4o integration with fallback mock)
Contributor wallet and earnings tracking with per-language rate plans
Weekly milestone payout calculation and summary for Orange Money disbursement
Public leaderboard ranking contributors by recording minutes and quality score
Admin statistics dashboard (recordings by status, transcriptions, language progress, user counts)
Multi-language UI (English + French) with next-intl, cookie-based locale switching
Multi-country, multi-language support (SL, GN, LR — Krio, Mende, Temne, Susu, Fula and others via ISO 639-3 codes)
featureInventory.evidence: prisma/schema.prisma:Country model (SL, GN, LR), Language model (kri, men, tem, sus...)

featureInventory.feature: Kay X automatic transcription field (auto-generates Krio transcript with confidence score)
Self-service GDPR account + data deletion (cascade deletes recordings from GCS + DB)
Password reset via email (nodemailer/Gmail with time-limited tokens)
Rate limiting on auth and upload endpoints (in-memory, per-IP, configurable window)
CI/CD pipeline: lint + test + build on push/PR (GitHub Actions with PostgreSQL service)
Seed scripts for demo data and admin users
Draft save and resume for transcribers
featureInventory.evidence: README claims this feature; no dedicated draft API route found (not verified in code)

sdgMapping.evidence: Building a speech corpus for Krio and other West African languages directly enables NLP/ASR tools, language technology education, and digital literacy in indigenous languages. Multi-language prompt system (prisma/schema.prisma:PromptCategory), recording/transcription pipeline (src/app/api/v2/speaker/, transcriber/), and open dataset export (src/app/api/v2/admin/export/route.ts) collectively advance quality education by preserving and digitising underrepresented languages.

Krio is the lingua franca of Sierra Leone (~8 million speakers); Mende, Temne, Susu, Fula are minority languages of Guinea, Liberia, and Sierra Leone. By creating open, high-quality speech data for these languages, KACCP directly reduces digital inequality — languages with no speech corpora are excluded from modern AI/NLP tools. prisma/schema.prisma:Language model includes ISO 639-3 codes for these underserved groups.
features: Multi-language support for West African minority languages
Orange Money payouts (SLE, GNF, LRD currencies)

sdgMapping.evidence: Contributors are paid per approved minute of recording via Orange Money (src/app/api/v2/admin/payouts/weekly/route.ts). The wallet system (prisma/schema.prisma:WalletTransaction, Payment; src/lib/infra/payments.ts) and per-language configurable rate plans create dignified economic opportunities for speakers and transcribers in Sierra Leone, Guinea, and Liberia.

The platform builds open data infrastructure for underrepresented languages — a form of digital public infrastructure (SDG 9.c). LJSpeech-format export standard (src/app/api/v2/admin/export/route.ts) makes corpus directly usable by ML researchers. Pluggable storage architecture (src/lib/infra/storage/) and open-source codebase enable other African language projects to fork/adapt.
features: Reusable platform architecture for other languages

scopeAnalysis.primaryFunction: KACCP is a crowdsourced speech corpus curation platform that collects, transcribes, reviews, and exports voice recordings for Krio and other low-resource West African languages, paying contributors via Orange Money and exporting data in open LJSpeech format for NLP/ASR research.
scopeAnalysis.existingCoverage: The DPG registry has NO direct equivalent — there is no approved DPG focused on low-resource African language speech corpus collection. The closest analogues are data collection platforms (KoboToolbox, CommCare) and crowdsourcing tools (Ushahidi), but none target speech/audio corpus curation for minority languages. This niche is genuinely underserved.
scopeAnalysis.uniqueValue: KACCP is the only open-source platform specifically designed for West African speech corpus collection with built-in contributor payment, multi-role workflow (speaker/transcriber/reviewer), and LJSpeech-compatible export. Its geographic specificity (Sierra Leone, Guinea, Liberia) and language focus (Krio, Mende, Temne, Susu, Fula) constitute genuine differentiation that no existing DPG provides.
scopeAnalysis.approvalAngle: KACCP fills a documented gap: low-resource African language speech data is a critical missing input for building inclusive AI. The platform is specifically engineered for West African contexts (Orange Money payments, local currencies SLE/GNF/LRD, Sierra Leone-specific prompt scenarios), is fully MIT-licensed and deployable by any organisation, and produces open datasets in a standard ML format. No other DPG addresses this space.

similarDPGs.name: KoboToolbox
similarDPGs.overlap: Both are data collection platforms with admin dashboards and multi-contributor workflows. KoboToolbox collects structured survey data; KACCP collects audio recordings.
similarDPGs.differentiator: KACCP is purpose-built for audio recording with timed assignments, audio-specific quality flags (NOISE, UNCLEAR), speaker label anonymisation, and LJSpeech-format ML export. KoboToolbox has no audio recording or NLP corpus features.
similarDPGs.leverageOpportunity: Reference KoboToolbox's field data collection methodology; KACCP could integrate with KoboToolbox for speaker metadata collection in field settings.

similarDPGs.name: The Ushahidi Platform
similarDPGs.overlap: Both use crowdsourcing to collect and manage data contributed by distributed communities.
similarDPGs.differentiator: Ushahidi focuses on incident mapping and crisis reporting. KACCP is a speech corpus pipeline with structured recording prompts, audio review, transcription assignments, and ML-ready exports.
similarDPGs.leverageOpportunity: Ushahidi's community coordination patterns are useful reference; KACCP could use Ushahidi for community engagement/reporting in field deployments.

similarDPGs.name: Cboard
similarDPGs.overlap: Both deal with speech/language accessibility for under-resourced groups.
similarDPGs.differentiator: Cboard is an AAC app for speech-impaired users; KACCP builds training data for speech recognition systems. Different problem spaces.
similarDPGs.leverageOpportunity: KACCP's corpus could ultimately feed speech technology that improves tools like Cboard for African languages.

similarDPGs.name: African Storybook
similarDPGs.overlap: Both focus on African language content creation and preservation.
similarDPGs.differentiator: African Storybook is a reading content platform (text/images); KACCP is a speech data collection pipeline. Complementary, not competitive.
similarDPGs.leverageOpportunity: African Storybook text content could serve as a source of prompt text for KACCP recordings, creating aligned text-audio corpora.

leverageRecommendations.description: Study KoboToolbox's contributor management patterns for large-scale field data collection. Their approach to data quality, offline-first design, and survey workflow has been validated at scale across Africa.
leverageRecommendations.impact: Saves 2–3 months of UX research; validated patterns for managing distributed contributors in low-connectivity environments.

leverageRecommendations.description: Partner with African Storybook to use their existing Krio/West African language texts as audio prompts in KACCP, creating aligned text-speech datasets.
leverageRecommendations.impact: Immediately expands the prompt library with culturally validated content; creates a natural data partnership between two complementary DPGs.

leverageRecommendations.description: Publish exported KACCP datasets to a CKAN instance as open data, making them discoverable and citable for ML researchers worldwide.
leverageRecommendations.impact: Dramatically increases dataset discoverability and academic citation; aligns with open data standards expected of a DPG.

status.name: SDG Relevance
status.explanation: KACCP has strong, implemented SDG alignment across SDG 4 (Quality Education), SDG 10 (Reduced Inequalities), SDG 8 (Decent Work), and SDG 9 (Innovation). The entire codebase — from the multi-language prompt system (prisma/schema.prisma:PromptCategory with 21 categories), to the recording/transcription pipeline, Orange Money payout system (src/app/api/v2/admin/payouts/weekly/route.ts), and LJSpeech export (src/app/api/v2/admin/export/route.ts) — directly serves the goal of building open, inclusive language technology for underserved West African communities. The geographic focus (Sierra Leone, Guinea, Liberia) and language specificity (ISO 639-3 codes: kri, men, tem, sus) demonstrate intent beyond generic tech. SDG alignment is evidenced in code, not just README claims.
status.recommendation: Formally document the SDG mapping in the README or a dedicated IMPACT.md file, citing specific implemented features as evidence. The DPGA typically requires a brief narrative for each claimed SDG. Registering datasets on platforms like Zenodo or Hugging Face Datasets Hub will demonstrate real-world SDG impact.
status.peerComparison: KoboToolbox (SDG 3,4,5,10,11,13) passes this criterion by demonstrating concrete data collection in humanitarian contexts. KACCP's approach is comparable — specific use case for a specific underserved population, with working infrastructure. The lack of a prior DPG in the speech corpus space is actually an advantage here: KACCP has no overlap problem.

status.name: Open Licensing
status.explanation: MIT License is present and complete at LICENSE.md with correct copyright: 'Copyright (c) 2026 Geneline-X'. The package.json declares '"license": "MIT"' consistent with the LICENSE file. The README includes an MIT license badge and a License section. No proprietary dependencies were identified that would create license conflicts — all major dependencies (Next.js, Prisma, Radix UI, Zod) are MIT or compatible open source licenses. The license text is complete and unmodified standard MIT.
status.recommendation: Minor: add a SPDX identifier comment to key source files (e.g., '// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT') for machine-readable license attribution. This is a best practice but not a blocker for DPG approval.
status.peerComparison: All reviewed peer DPGs (KoboToolbox: BSD, CommCare: BSD-3, Ushahidi: LGPL) have valid OSI-approved licenses. KACCP meets the same standard. MIT is among the most permissive, which strengthens the case that the software can be freely reused and adapted.

status.name: Clear Ownership
status.explanation: Ownership is clearly and consistently documented: 'Copyright (c) 2026 Geneline-X' in LICENSE.md; 'author: Geneline-X <info@geneline-x.net>' in package.json; MAINTAINERS.md lists Geneline-X as primary maintainer with GitHub (https://github.com/Geneline-X) and email (info@geneline-x.net); README credits 'Developed by Geneline-X'; CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md enforcement contact is info@geneline-x.net. Geneline-X is a verifiable organisation with a public website (geneline-x.net) and GitHub org. All files are internally consistent — no conflicting ownership claims found.
status.recommendation: Add a GOVERNANCE.md describing how major decisions are made, roadmap ownership, and contributor promotion path. MAINTAINERS.md currently only lists the Geneline-X team generically — naming at least one or two individual leads strengthens the bus-factor case for long-term sustainability.
status.peerComparison: KoboToolbox (owned by Kobo Inc), CommCare (Dimagi), Ushahidi (Ushahidi Inc) all have clear organizational ownership. KACCP matches this pattern with a named organization, contact email, and GitHub org.

status.name: Platform Independence
status.explanation: KACCP demonstrates thoughtful platform independence. The primary vendor dependency is Google Cloud Storage — but this is fully abstracted behind a StorageProvider interface (src/lib/infra/storage/provider.ts) with two concrete implementations: GCSStorageProvider (production) and LocalStorageProvider (development/testing). Switching providers requires only changing the STORAGE_PROVIDER environment variable. The database layer uses Prisma ORM (src/lib/infra/db/prisma.ts) — PostgreSQL by default but Prisma supports MySQL, SQLite, MongoDB. The OpenAI integration (src/lib/infra/ai.ts) falls back gracefully to a mock when OPENAI_API_KEY is absent. The CI pipeline (ci.yml) explicitly sets STORAGE_PROVIDER=local, proving local-only operation works. Next.js, Node.js, and PostgreSQL are all open-source cross-platform tools.
status.recommendation: The only remaining GCS lock-in is that GCS-specific gs:// URI format is parsed throughout the codebase (src/lib/infra/gcs.ts:parseGsUri). Consider abstracting this into the StorageProvider so URIs are provider-agnostic. Also worth adding: a documented path to replace OpenAI with an open-source LLM (e.g., Whisper for transcription suggestions).
status.peerComparison: KoboToolbox uses Kobo's own cloud but publishes a self-hosted Docker image. CommCare uses AWS but has self-hosted options. KACCP's pluggable storage abstraction actually exceeds most peer DPGs in explicit platform independence at the code level.

status.name: Documentation
status.explanation: KACCP has strong documentation coverage. The README (184 lines) covers: project description, audience, feature list, folder structure, prerequisites (Node 20+, PostgreSQL 15+, GCS bucket or local storage), step-by-step installation (6 numbered steps with exact commands), usage guide, architecture description, contributing, and license. CONTRIBUTING.md covers setup, workflow, PR guidelines, bug reporting, and security disclosure. SECURITY.md, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, MAINTAINERS.md, and PRIVACY.md are all present and substantive. The .env.example file documents all required environment variables. The Prisma schema is extensively commented inline (every model and key field has comments). The CI configuration (ci.yml) serves as deployable documentation. One gap: no API reference documentation (no OpenAPI/Swagger) and no user guide with screenshots.
status.recommendation: Add an API reference (even a simple markdown table of all endpoints with parameters) — the v2 API has 20+ endpoints with no aggregate documentation. Consider a DEPLOYMENT.md covering production deployment with GCS. Adding a quick-start Docker Compose would significantly lower the barrier to self-hosting for other organizations.
status.peerComparison: KoboToolbox has extensive documentation at docs.kobotoolbox.org. CommCare has developer docs. KACCP's in-repo documentation is solid but lacks the API reference and deployment guide that mature DPGs provide. This is a medium-priority improvement.

status.name: Data Extraction
status.explanation: KACCP collects significant user data (voice recordings, transcriptions, user profiles, payment records) and has implemented data extraction mechanisms. The admin export endpoint (src/app/api/v2/admin/export/route.ts) supports JSON and CSV in LJSpeech format with speaker anonymisation via speaker labels (speaker_0001, etc. — never personal names). The export properly filters PII: speaker labels replace user IDs, and personal data (email, phone) is excluded from the export schema. The export is accessible to admins with bulk export (all recordings for a language) and preview mode. The ExportRecord model (prisma/schema.prisma) tracks what has been exported for incremental exports. The DELETE /api/v2/account route (src/app/api/v2/account/route.ts) provides individual data portability/erasure. CSV output uses the open, non-proprietary LJSpeech pipe-delimited format.
status.recommendation: Add a user-facing data export (GET /api/v2/account/export) so individual contributors can download their own recordings and transcriptions — currently only admin export exists for dataset purposes. This is a GDPR data portability right gap. Also consider publishing the exported dataset publicly (Zenodo, Hugging Face Hub) once sufficient data is collected, which would strengthen the DPG case.
status.peerComparison: KoboToolbox has XLS, CSV, JSON, KML export with full API access — considered a gold standard for this criterion. KACCP's export is well-implemented but limited to admin-only access. The LJSpeech format is a recognized ML standard, which is a strong positive signal.

status.name: Privacy & Legal Compliance
status.explanation: KACCP has comprehensive privacy implementation. PRIVACY.md covers data categories (with legal basis), biometric data protections for voice recordings, user rights (access, rectification, erasure, portability, consent withdrawal), data retention policy, age restriction (18+), data transfer details (GCS/Google LLC, standard contractual clauses), and security measures. Implementation in code: bcrypt password hashing with 10 salt rounds (src/lib/infra/auth/auth.ts:hashPassword); JWT with 7-day expiry (signJwt); role-based access control (ADMIN/SPEAKER/TRANSCRIBER/REVIEWER); rate limiting on auth and upload endpoints (src/lib/infra/rate-limit.ts); signed URL expiry for audio files (15 min write, 1 hour read); speaker anonymisation in exports; full GDPR account deletion cascade (src/app/api/v2/account/route.ts). The recording model stores consentGiven boolean (prisma/schema.prisma:Recording.consentGiven). SECURITY.md provides a vulnerability disclosure policy with 48-hour acknowledgment and 90-day coordinated disclosure.
status.recommendation: Two gaps: (1) The register route (src/app/api/auth/register/route.ts) does not capture explicit consent at registration time despite PRIVACY.md claiming this. Consider adding a 'consentToTerms: boolean' field validated server-side. (2) No CSRF protection is visible in the Next.js API routes. Next.js App Router has some CSRF protection but explicit CSRF tokens for state-changing endpoints would strengthen this. Also: the email provider hardcodes Gmail (src/lib/infra/email.ts) — consider making the SMTP provider configurable for deployments without Gmail.
status.peerComparison: KoboToolbox and CommCare both have detailed privacy policies and GDPR compliance. KACCP's privacy implementation is notably strong for an early-stage project, particularly the biometric data (voice recording) handling with anonymised speaker labels and explicit consent mechanism.

status.name: Standards & Best Practices
status.explanation: Code quality is solid. TypeScript strict mode is enabled (tsconfig.json: strict: true). ESLint is configured and runs in CI (eslint.config.mjs with next/core-web-vitals and typescript rules). Test suite: 4 test files with 25+ real test cases using Vitest — tests cover GET /audio/:id (9 cases), GET /reviewer/recordings (auth, pagination), approve and reject routes. Tests run in CI. The code is consistently structured: Next.js App Router conventions, clear separation of concerns (lib/infra/, lib/domain/, components/), consistent error handling, parameterised Prisma queries (no raw SQL injection risk). Standard data formats: LJSpeech CSV/JSON export, ISO 639-3 language codes, ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country codes, standard JWT (HS256). Lock file (bun.lock and package-lock.json) present. Note: next.config.ts has ignoreBuildErrors: true which is a significant concern — TypeScript errors are silently ignored in builds.
status.recommendation: Critical: remove 'typescript.ignoreBuildErrors: true' from next.config.ts — this defeats the purpose of TypeScript strict mode. Fix the underlying type errors instead. Expand test coverage: only reviewer and audio routes have tests; the transcriber submission, speaker upload, and admin export routes are untested. Consider adding integration tests. A CHANGELOG.md would complete the standard documentation suite. Adding OpenAPI/Swagger spec for the v2 API would meet the API documentation standard.
status.peerComparison: KoboToolbox has extensive tests and CI. CommCare has 90%+ test coverage. KACCP's test coverage is notably sparse — covering only 4 of 20+ API routes. This is the weakest standards aspect but the framework is in place.

status.name: Do No Harm by Design
status.explanation: KACCP's harm profile is relatively low (speech data collection platform, not social/communication). Implemented safeguards: rate limiting on all auth endpoints prevents brute-force (15 req/15min) and upload abuse (20 req/min) (src/lib/infra/rate-limit.ts); RBAC prevents data access across roles; recording access is speaker-restricted (speakers can only access their own recordings, verified in test at src/__tests__/api/v2/audio.test.ts:line 94); voice recordings are stored with speaker labels not personal data (anonymisation by design); age restriction (18+) in PRIVACY.md; SECURITY.md with responsible disclosure; CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md for community safety; bcrypt passwords; JWT expiry; account deletion cascade. The platform explicitly excludes PII from exported datasets. No user-generated public content (no social features, no public posts) that would require content moderation.
status.recommendation: Two medium-priority gaps: (1) The consent field on recordings (Recording.consentGiven) defaults to true in the schema without explicit capture at recording time — should require active opt-in. (2) No formal content safety measure for audio content (e.g., speakers uploading inappropriate audio). Consider adding a flag mechanism for reviewers to flag audio containing harmful content in addition to audio quality flags. The AI transcription (OpenAI) should have a note about data sent to external services in the privacy policy.
status.peerComparison: Ushahidi has content moderation features for crisis reporting. KoboToolbox has data security and access controls. KACCP's harm profile is lighter (purpose-specific data collection vs public social platform), and its protections are proportionate and well-implemented for its use case.

final_recommendation: KACCP is a strong DPG candidate with a clear gap-filling value proposition in the open-data ecosystem. It is the only known open-source platform specifically designed for West African speech corpus collection, targeting languages with no existing approved DPG coverage. The codebase is production-quality: TypeScript strict mode, Prisma ORM, RBAC, bcrypt, rate limiting, GCS signed URLs, and a working CI pipeline. The privacy implementation is exemplary for an early-stage project. The primary weaknesses are (1) sparse test coverage beyond reviewer routes, (2) TypeScript build errors being silenced in next.config.ts, (3) no user-facing data export for individual contributors, and (4) no API reference documentation. None of these are blockers for DPG approval, but all should be addressed in the next 4-6 weeks. Priority actions: fix ignoreBuildErrors, expand test coverage to transcriber/admin routes, add a user data export endpoint, publish an API reference, and push a public dataset to Hugging Face Hub to demonstrate real-world SDG impact.

priorityActions.action: Remove 'typescript.ignoreBuildErrors: true' from next.config.ts and fix all TypeScript type errors. This silently defeats strict type checking in production builds.
Add user-facing data export endpoint (GET /api/v2/account/export) returning the user's own recordings and transcriptions — required for GDPR data portability compliance.
Expand test coverage to cover transcriber submit, admin export, and speaker upload routes. Currently only 4 of 20+ routes are tested.
Add explicit consent capture at registration (server-side validation of consentToTerms boolean) to match PRIVACY.md claims.
Write API reference documentation listing all /api/v2/ endpoints with parameters, auth requirements, and example responses.
Publish first dataset release to Hugging Face Hub or Zenodo with proper CC-BY licensing to demonstrate real-world SDG impact and open data contribution.
Add GOVERNANCE.md and name individual project leads in MAINTAINERS.md to reduce bus-factor concerns for DPG reviewers.
Make email SMTP provider configurable (not hardcoded to Gmail) so self-hosting organizations can use their own mail server.
Add a DEPLOYMENT.md with production setup instructions (including GCS configuration, environment variables checklist, and scaling notes).
Add explicit CSRF protection to state-changing API endpoints beyond Next.js defaults.
Add SPDX license identifiers to source files for machine-readable license compliance.
Add a CHANGELOG.md tracking version history and changes.

phases.label: Weeks 1-2
items: Remove ignoreBuildErrors from next.config.ts and fix TypeScript errors
Add explicit consent capture at registration with server-side validation
Write API reference documentation for all /api/v2/ endpoints

phases.label: Weeks 3-4
items: Expand test coverage to transcriber, admin export, and speaker upload routes
Add GOVERNANCE.md and name individual maintainers
Publish initial Krio dataset to Hugging Face Hub or Zenodo
Add DEPLOYMENT.md with production setup instructions
Make SMTP provider configurable via environment variables
